measurements: Overall width of Garbha pavilion is 14 units (aṃśa).
Width of sanctum (garbhagrha) can be 2 parts (aṃśa).
Sanctum width can be double the width of the icon's pedestal (pīṭha).
Sanctum width can be equal to the height of the image (pratimā).
Sanctum width can be one-third the width of the temple (prāsāda).
In a śrīviśāla temple, sanctum width is half the total building width.
Sanctum width is four-tenths of total temple width in a 10-part division.
Image height can be derived from sanctum height.
Sanctum wall thickness can be one-third sanctum width.
Altar (bali pīṭha) height/width equal to half sanctum width.
In a Sukhavaha temple, sanctum width is 4/10 of ground floor width.
In an Attendant's shrine, sanctum width is half total shrine width.
For a Bhadrakita temple, sanctum width is four-tenths total temple width.
Minimum height of a Nāgara Liṅga is half sanctum width.
Minimum height of a Drāviḍa Liṅga is 10/21 of sanctum width.
Sanctum width can be one-third total temple width.
Pavilion (maṇḍapa) width can equal sanctum width.
In a 9-part plan (13 cubits total), sanctum width is 4 parts.
In a 15-part plan (15 cubits total), sanctum width is 6 parts.
In a Bhadrakoṣṭha temple, sanctum is 3 parts (aṃśa) wide.
relationships: Garbha (ritual deposit) is placed at temple summit (śikhara).
Garbha (ritual deposit) is placed on lateral walls (pārśvabhitti) in a palace.
Garbha (ritual deposit) is placed in centre (madhya) of a pavilion.
Garbha (ritual deposit) is placed under surmounting lotus (padma) in a hall.
Garbha (ritual deposit) is placed in southern part of a house site.
Foundation deposit (garbhanyāsa) is placed beneath wall south of entrance.
Foundation deposit is placed under plinth (adhiṣṭhāna).
Foundation deposit can be placed under stereobate (upapīṭha).
Foundation deposit is placed to the right of entrance (dvāra).
Foundation deposit may be placed under pillar to right of door.
Foundation deposit may be placed at corner of second pillar.
Foundation deposit may be placed at bottom of pillar in south.
Foundation deposit can be located under pratistambha (pillar).
Foundation deposit is located under Homastambha (sacrificial pillar).
Liṅga or image is placed in center of sanctum, displaced NE.
Garbhakuṭa is located above two nasikas on gable.
Interior wall (antarabhitti) is located around exterior of sanctum.
Secondary doors (bhugarbha dvāra) can be located on specific deity squares.
Garbha pavilion is supported by 208 pillars (stambha).
Ratikānta gateway contains a central sanctum (garbhagrha).
Gold Srivatsa symbol is part of hemagarbha hall deposit.
Caste-specific deposit (jāṭi garbha) is part of owner's main deposit.
Casket (garbhanyāsa pātra) is central component of deposit ritual.
Precious stones are part of foundation deposit arrangement.
Foundation deposit ritual is a type of consecration ceremony (pratiṣṭhā karma).
Rules for deposit apply to temples of gods (deva ālaya).
Rules for deposit apply to houses of twice-born classes (dvija gr̥ha).
Foundation deposit ritual is specified for temples of Īśa.
Act of laying deposit is a ritual action (kriya) with prohibitions.
Following finial consecration, architect consecrates sanctum.
Garbha for any building is determined by activity (kriya) performed.
Sudra deposit is also suitable for Vaiśya caste.
Hall for gold embryo ritual requires a consecration ritual.
Hemagarbha hall deposit also applies to tulābhāra pavilion.
contextual_info: Garbha has three primary meanings: ritual deposit, architectural sanctum, and building type.
Ritual deposit is central to construction consecration ceremonies.
Sanctum (garbhagrha) is the innermost chamber housing the main deity.
Square pavilion type is suitable for gods, brahmins, and kings.
Deposit contents vary by caste and building function.
Deposit placement varies by building type and location.
Dimensional rules for sanctum are proportional to other elements.
Foundation deposit ritual involves specific mantras and deities.
Geometric diagrams (padmagarbha, vipragarbha) use square grids.
Layout line (garbhasūtra) is a conceptual planning guideline.
Circular (vr̥tta) and square (caturasra) sanctum plans exist.
Pillar counts define various pavilion types.
Material specifications include precious metals for ritual objects.
Iconographic features are integrated with architectural elements.
Structural relationships define temple and pavilion composition.
